section.title: The Example of Christ's Suffering
section.sectionRationale: Peter's teaching on submission reaches its deepest point with household servants who suffer unjustly, and there he lifts their eyes to Christ. This passage joins a practical call to patient endurance with the letter's richest meditation on the cross, drawing on Isaiah 53 to show Christ as both the example to follow and the Savior who bore our sins.
section.thesis: When believers suffer unjustly for doing good, they follow in the footsteps of Christ, who committed no sin yet suffered silently, entrusting Himself to God, and who bore our sins in His body on the tree so that we might die to sin and live to righteousness, healed by His wounds and returned to the Shepherd of our souls.
section.frameNote: Three movements: the call to endure unjust suffering, verses 18 to 20; Christ our example under suffering, verses 21 to 23; and Christ our sin-bearing Savior, verses 24 to 25.

verses.text: Servants, {{hypotasso|submit yourselves}} to your masters with all respect, not only to those who are good and gentle, but even to those who are unreasonable.
For if anyone endures the pain of unjust suffering because he is conscious of God, this is to be commended.
How is it to your credit if you are beaten for doing wrong and you endure it? But if you suffer for doing good and you endure it, this is commendable before God.
For to this you were called, because Christ also suffered for you, leaving you an {{hypogrammos|example}}, that you should follow in His footsteps:
“He committed no sin, and no deceit was found in His mouth.”
When they heaped abuse on Him, He did not retaliate; when He suffered, He made no threats, but entrusted Himself to Him who judges justly.

units.label: Christ our sin-bearing Savior
units.note: by His stripes you are healed

verses.text: He Himself bore our sins in His body on the {{xylon|tree}}, so that we might die to sin and live to righteousness. “By His stripes you are {{iaomai|healed}}.”
For “you were like sheep going astray,” but now you have returned to the {{poimen|Shepherd}} and {{episkopos|Overseer}} of your souls.

hypotasso.gloss: To submit, to place oneself under. Here household servants are to submit to their masters with all respect, even harsh ones. As throughout the letter, this is voluntary submission offered out of reverence for God, not a claim that the master's cruelty is right. It sets the stage for the example of Christ.

hypogrammos.gloss: A pattern to be traced over, like the copy a child follows when learning to write. Christ leaves us such a pattern in His suffering, that we might follow in His footsteps. The word pictures His life as the model script the believer copies, especially in patient endurance of wrong.

xylon.gloss: Wood, a tree, here the cross. Peter deliberately calls the cross the tree, echoing Deuteronomy 21:23, cursed is everyone hung on a tree. Christ bore our sins on the tree, taking the curse in our place, so the word carries the weight of substitution and the fulfilment of the law's curse.

iaomai.gloss: To heal, to make whole. Quoting Isaiah 53, Peter says by His wounds you are healed. The healing is chiefly the deep restoration of sinners to God, forgiveness and new life, wrought through Christ's suffering. His stripes are the cost by which our sin-sick souls are made well.

poimen.gloss: Shepherd, one who tends and guards the flock. Straying like lost sheep, believers have now returned to Christ the Shepherd of their souls. The image runs through Scripture, from the Lord as Israel's shepherd to Jesus the Good Shepherd, and anticipates the Chief Shepherd of chapter five.

episkopos.gloss: Overseer, guardian, one who watches over. Paired with Shepherd, it describes Christ's tender, vigilant care for His people. He does not merely rescue wandering sheep; He watches over their souls with constant, protective oversight, guarding them safely home.

basic.context: Peter speaks to household servants, many of whom had cruel masters. He tells them to submit respectfully, even to the harsh ones, and says that enduring unjust suffering for doing good is commendable before God. Then he lifts their eyes to Jesus. Christ suffered for us, leaving us an example to follow: He never sinned, never lied, never hit back, but trusted God who judges justly. And He did more than set an example. He bore our sins in His body on the cross so we could die to sin and live for righteousness. By His wounds we are healed. We were straying sheep, but now we have come home to the Shepherd who watches over our souls.
basic.hermeneutics: This passage holds two truths together carefully. Christ is our example in suffering, verses 21 to 23, and Christ is our substitute, bearing our sins in a way we never could, verses 24 to 25. We must not reduce the cross to a mere moral lesson; He did what only He could do. Yet neither is His suffering only substitution; it is also the pattern His people trace when they suffer for doing good. Both are true, and the passage refuses to lose either.
basic.originalLanguages: Servants hypotasso, submit, to masters even when harsh. Christ leaves a hypogrammos, a pattern to trace, in His suffering. He bore our sins on the xylon, the tree, and by His wounds we are iaomai, healed. Straying sheep have returned to the poimen, the Shepherd, and episkopos, the Overseer, of their souls.
basic.typology: The whole passage is soaked in Isaiah 53, the Suffering Servant who is silent before His accusers, bears the sins of many, and heals by His wounds. Calling the cross the tree evokes Deuteronomy 21, the curse of hanging on a tree, taken by Christ. And the returning sheep fulfils the shepherd promises of Ezekiel 34 and Psalm 23, answered in Jesus.
basic.christ: Christ stands at the centre as both example and substitute. Sinless and silent under abuse, He entrusted Himself to God, showing us how to suffer. But then He did what we cannot: He bore our sins in His body on the tree, took the curse, and healed us by His wounds. He is the Suffering Servant of Isaiah 53 and the Shepherd and Overseer to whom lost sheep return.
basic.god: God is the one who judges justly, to whom Christ entrusted Himself, and before whom patient endurance of unjust suffering is commendable. He is just, so wrongs will be righted, and He is the God who accomplished salvation through the suffering of His Son. He is the Shepherd God who brings straying sheep home through Christ.
basic.crossReferences: Isaiah 53 is the great backdrop, the Servant who bears our sins and heals by His wounds. Deuteronomy 21 speaks of the curse on one hung on a tree. Ezekiel 34 promises God to shepherd His scattered sheep. And Romans 6 speaks of dying to sin and living to God, as Peter says here.
basic.oneStory: Israel's prophets foretold a Servant who would suffer silently and bear the sins of many, and a Shepherd God who would gather His straying flock. In Christ both come true at the cross: He takes the curse on the tree, heals by His wounds, and draws wandering sheep home. The suffering of God's people, when it is for doing good, now traces the footsteps of that Servant and Shepherd.

context.literary: The passage completes the household teaching begun with citizens, addressing servants, and then rises into a hymn-like meditation on Christ's suffering built almost entirely from Isaiah 53. Verses 18 to 20 give the exhortation; verse 21 pivots to Christ as example; verses 22 to 25 unfold His sinless endurance and sin-bearing death in a series of clauses drawn from the prophet. It is the theological summit of the letter's teaching on suffering.
context.historical: Household servants formed a large part of the early church and often served masters who could punish them at will, sometimes unjustly. Peter neither endorses the institution nor calls for revolt; he addresses believers caught within it, dignifying their patient endurance as sharing in Christ's own path. The passage would have spoken with special force to the powerless.
context.canonical: This is one of the New Testament's fullest expositions of Isaiah 53, reading the Suffering Servant directly of Christ. It brings together substitutionary atonement, moral example, and the shepherd theme, and it feeds the wider biblical portrait of the cross as both the place where sin is borne and the pattern for cross-shaped discipleship.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To call believers who suffer unjustly for doing good to patient endurance after the example of Christ, and to ground that call in the deeper truth that Christ suffered as their sin-bearing Savior, taking their sins on the tree and returning them as straying sheep to the Shepherd of their souls.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: Verses 18 to 21 prescribe: submit, endure, follow. Verses 22 to 25 describe what Christ did, and there the description of His sin-bearing death is not something believers imitate but something they receive. The example is to be followed; the atonement is to be trusted. Reading either as the other distorts the passage.

debates.question: Is Christ's suffering here mainly an example or mainly substitution?

views.label: Both, held together
views.case: Verses 21 to 23 present Christ as the pattern for enduring wrong, while verses 24 to 25 present Him as the substitute who bore our sins on the tree. Peter deliberately joins the two: the Savior we trust is also the model we follow.

views.label: Substitution as the foundation of the example
views.case: Others stress that the sin-bearing of verse 24 is primary and unique; the example only has power because He is first the Savior. We follow His footsteps because He has first borne our sins and made us His own.

debates.bearing: Both guard against reducing the cross to a mere moral lesson. Christ's unique, sin-bearing death is the ground; His patient endurance is the pattern His redeemed people trace in their own suffering.

debates.question: What healing is meant by by His stripes you are healed, verse 24?

views.label: Chiefly spiritual healing from sin
views.case: The context is bearing sins and dying to sin, so the healing is the deep restoration of sinners to God, forgiveness and new life. Straying sheep are made well by returning to the Shepherd of their souls.

views.label: A healing that anticipates full restoration
views.case: Others note that Christ's atonement secures the whole person's final wholeness, so while the present focus is spiritual, it points toward the complete healing of body and soul in the resurrection.

debates.bearing: Both keep the primary sense spiritual, the healing of sin's deadly wound, while affirming that this restoration will one day be complete. It is not a promise of guaranteed physical cure now.

study.originalLanguages: Servants hypotasso, submit, even to unreasonable masters, and patient endurance of unjust suffering for God's sake is commendable. Christ then becomes the model: He leaves a hypogrammos, a copybook pattern to trace over, and He suffered without sin, without deceit, without retaliation, entrusting Himself to the just Judge. Yet He is more than pattern. He bore our sins in His body on the xylon, the tree, taking the law's curse, so that we might die to sin and live to righteousness, and by His wounds we are iaomai, healed, restored from sin's deadly sickness. Once straying sheep, we have returned to the poimen and episkopos, the Shepherd and Overseer, of our souls, the one who both gathers and guards His flock.

typology.fulfillment: Christ, who committed no sin, was silent before His accusers, bore the sins of many, and healed by His wounds, exactly as Isaiah foretold.

typology.type: The curse of hanging on a tree, Deuteronomy 21:23
typology.fulfillment: Christ bearing our sins on the tree, taking upon Himself the curse due to us.

typology.type: The Shepherd gathering straying sheep, Ezekiel 34 and Psalm 23
typology.fulfillment: Christ the Shepherd and Overseer of souls, to whom the wandering flock has now returned.

study.christ: Christ is presented as the Suffering Servant of Isaiah 53, and the passage will not let us choose between example and atonement. As example, He is sinless, truthful, and non-retaliating, entrusting Himself to the God who judges justly, so that suffering believers have footsteps to follow. As Savior, He bore our sins in His body on the tree, took the curse, and healed us by His wounds, doing what no example could accomplish. He is the crucified sin-bearer and the risen Shepherd and Overseer to whom straying sinners return and under whose care they are kept.
study.god: God is revealed as the just Judge to whom Christ entrusted Himself, the one before whom patient endurance of unjust suffering is truly commendable. Because He judges justly, believers need not retaliate; they can leave their case in His hands. He is also the God who accomplished redemption through the suffering of His Son, and who through Christ shepherds and oversees the souls of His people, bringing wandering sheep safely home. He is just, saving, and watchful over His flock.

crossReferences.note: The Servant bears our sins, is silent before His accusers, and heals by His wounds, the passage Peter quotes throughout verses 22 to 25.
Cursed is everyone hung on a tree, the curse Christ took when He bore our sins on the tree.
God promises to seek and shepherd His scattered sheep, fulfilled in Christ the Shepherd of souls.
Consider yourselves dead to sin and alive to God, matching Peter's die to sin and live to righteousness.

study.oneStory: The prophets foretold a Servant who would suffer in silence and bear the sins of many, and a Shepherd God who would gather His straying flock. At the cross both promises meet: Christ takes the curse on the tree, heals by His wounds, and turns wandering sheep toward home. The unjust suffering of God's people, when borne for doing good, now traces the footsteps of that Servant, and their homecoming to the Shepherd of their souls is a foretaste of the whole story's end, the flock safe under its King.

quiz.q: What kind of suffering does Peter say is commendable before God?

options.text: Enduring unjust suffering for doing good, out of consciousness of God.
options.note: Verses 19 to 20: suffering for doing good and enduring it is what God commends.

options.text: Suffering that comes as punishment for doing wrong.
options.note: Peter says there is no credit in enduring a beating deserved for wrongdoing.

options.text: Any hardship at all, regardless of its cause.
options.note: The commendable suffering is specifically for doing good, not suffering in general.

options.text: Patient endurance of mistreatment while doing right.
options.note: True and close, though the answer should include that it is done out of consciousness of God.

quiz.q: In what way did Christ leave us an example in His suffering?

options.text: He committed no sin and did not retaliate, but entrusted Himself to God who judges justly.
options.note: Verses 22 to 23: sinless, silent, non-retaliating, trusting the just Judge.

options.text: He avoided suffering wherever He could.
options.note: He suffered for us; the example is how He bore it, not that He escaped it.

options.text: He fought back against those who wronged Him.
options.note: The opposite: He did not retaliate or threaten.

options.text: He bore mistreatment patiently, leaving a pattern to follow.
options.note: True, He leaves a pattern to trace, though the answer should name His sinlessness and trust in God.

quiz.q: What did Christ do that goes beyond leaving an example?

options.text: He bore our sins in His body on the tree so we might die to sin and live to righteousness.
options.note: Verse 24: substitution, not mere example, is the heart of the cross here.

options.text: He showed that good people sometimes suffer.
options.note: That misses the unique, sin-bearing work only He could accomplish.

options.text: He healed us by His wounds, restoring us from sin's deadly sickness.
options.note: True, verse 24, and part of the sin-bearing work, though the answer should name that He bore our sins.

options.text: He taught a new philosophy of endurance.
options.note: The cross is atonement, not merely a teaching about suffering.

quiz.q: Why does Peter call the cross the tree?

options.text: To echo the curse on anyone hung on a tree, which Christ took in our place.
options.note: Verse 24 draws on Deuteronomy 21:23; Christ bore the curse for us.

options.text: Simply because the cross was made of wood.
options.note: It was wooden, but the deeper point is the curse language of Deuteronomy.

options.text: To soften the horror of crucifixion.
options.note: The term intensifies, not softens, pointing to the curse Christ bore.

options.text: To connect His death with the law's judgment on sin.
options.note: True to the sense, though the answer should name the specific curse of hanging on a tree.

quiz.q: How does Peter describe the believers' return to Christ?

options.text: As straying sheep who have returned to the Shepherd and Overseer of their souls.
options.note: Verse 25: lost sheep brought home to the Shepherd who watches over them.

options.text: As citizens who have joined a new nation.
options.note: The image here is sheep returning to a shepherd, not a change of citizenship.

options.text: As wanderers now cared for and guarded by Christ.
options.note: True to the sense, though the answer should name the Shepherd and Overseer of souls.

options.text: As people who never really went astray.
options.note: Peter says they were like sheep going astray before their return.

questions: Peter dignifies the patient endurance of those with little power. Where are you suffering something unfair, and what would it mean to entrust it to God who judges justly?
Christ did not retaliate when abused. When you are wronged or insulted, what is your first instinct, and how might Christ's example reshape it?
He bore your sins in His body on the tree. Take a moment to name a specific sin He carried for you there. How does that move you?
By His wounds you are healed. In what part of your soul do you most need the healing that only Christ's suffering can bring?
You were a straying sheep who has returned to the Shepherd of your souls. Where are you tempted to wander now, and how does the Shepherd's watchful care draw you back?

translationNotes.note: Example translates hypogrammos, the copybook pattern a child traces to learn to write, picturing Christ's life as the model believers copy.
Verses 22 to 25 quote and echo Isaiah 53 repeatedly; by His stripes you are healed comes from Isaiah 53:5, chiefly meaning spiritual restoration from sin.
In His body on the tree uses xylon, wood or tree, evoking Deuteronomy 21:23 and the curse Christ bore in our place.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for 1 Peter 2:18-25. No dashes appear in this movement's text.
